content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

January 9, 1992

Ms. Jayne F. Herman
Office of the County Counsel
1195 Third Street, Room 301
Napa, California 94559-3001

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-91-554

Dear Ms. Herman:

This letter is in response to your request for advice
regarding the duties and responsibilities of Kathryn J. Winter
under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform
Act (the "Act").¹ Your request is general in nature; accordingly,
we are providing you with informal assistance pursuant to the
provisions of Regulation 18329.²

QUESTION

Do the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act require
Planning Commission Chairperson Kathryn J. Winter to disqualify
herself from participating in decisions regarding an inclusionary
housing ordinance which would encourage the provision of
affordable housing in connection with the development of
residential projects?

CONCLUSION

The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act require
Chairperson Winter to disqualify herself from participating in

___________________________
¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento CA 95804-0807 ● (916) 322-5660

Our File No. I-91-554
Page 2


decisions regarding the proposed ordinance if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the pending governmental decisions will have a
material financial effect on her economic interests, as discussed
below.

FACTS

Kathryn J. Winter is Chairperson of the Napa County
Conservation, Development and Planning Commission. Chairperson
Winter's husband, Mick Winter, is employed as a systems analyst
for The Doctors' Company ("Doctors"), a physician-owned nonprofit
medical malpractice insurer. Doctors, a company with annual
revenues of approximately one hundred fifty million dollars, has
offices in Napa County. Among its assets, Doctors posses
approximately 11 acres of land in the unincorporated portion of
Napa County, including a five-acre site upon which its new offices
are being constructed and approximately six acres of undeveloped
land. Doctors has no current plans to develop or sell its
undeveloped land.

The housing element of the Napa County general plan states
that the provision of affordable housing in the county is to be
encouraged in connection with the development of residential
projects. The Napa County Conservation, Development and Planning
Commission is currently scheduled to consider whether to recommend
to the Napa County Board of Supervisors that an inclusionary
housing ordinance be developed and adopted as part of a
comprehensive response to this aspect of the county's general
plan. Such a recommendation, if made, could include endorsement
of a model ordinance requiring some or all of the following: (1)
that a certain percentage of all new residential units constructed
in a project be "affordable" as that term is defined in the
ordinance; (2) that a fee be paid to an affordable housing fund
in lieu of the actual construction of affordable units; or
(3) that construction of a certain number of affordable units take
place off-site. In addition, the Napa County Conservation,
Development and Planning Commission is likely to recommend
creation of additional sources of funding for affordable housing
through means such as assessment of linkage fees from developers
of agricultural, commercial and industrial projects based upon
project-generated employment, imposition of a business license
tax, or increase in documentary transfer or sales taxes. If
Doctors ever develops its undeveloped property located in the
unincorporated area of Napa County, such property would be subject
to any ordinance ultimately adopted by the County of Napa Board of
Supervisors.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making,
participating in, or using their official position to influence a
governmental decision in which they know or have reason to know
they have a financial interest. Chairperson Winter is a public
official. (Section 82048.)

Our File No. I-91-554
Page 3



Financial Interests

An official has a financial interest in a decision within the
meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally, on the official, or on a
member of the official's immediate family,³ or on:

     (c)  Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.

                           Section 87103.

Chairperson Winter has a potentially disqualifying economic
interest under Section 87103(c). This interest must be evaluated
to determine whether she must disqualify herself from
participating in governmental decisions regarding the proposed
ordinance.

Section 82030 defines "income," in pertinent part, as
follows:

     "Income" means, except as provided in subdivision
(b), a payment received, including but not limited
to any salary, wage, advance, dividend, interest,
rent, proceeds from any sale, gift, including any
gift of food or beverage, loan, forgiveness or
payment of indebtedness received by the filer,
reimbursement for expenses, per diem,
contribution to an insurance or pension program
paid by any person other than an employer, and
including any community property interest in the
income of a spouse.

Thus the salary earned by Mick Winter from his employer,
Doctors, would be considered income to Chairperson Winter, and
Doctors would then be a source of income as contemplated by
Section 87103(c), quoted above. We are assuming that Doctors paid
Mr. Winter at least $500 in the last twelve months, so that
Chairperson Winter would have a community property interest of at
least $250 in her husband's salary.

_________________________

³ An official's "immediate family" are his or her spouse and
dependent children. (Section 82029.)

Our File No. I-91-554
Page 4


Making, Participating in Making, or Attempting to Influence a
Governmental Decision

A public official makes a governmental decision or
participates in the making of a governmental decision whenever the
public official votes on a matter, directs or approves of an agency to course
of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of
the agency. (Regulation 18700(b).)" Additionally, a public
official participates in a governmental decision when, acting
within the authority of his or her position, the public official:

(1) Negotiates, without significant
substantive review, with a governmental entity or
private person regarding the decision; or

(2) Advises or makes recommendations to the
decision-maker, either directly or without
significant intervening substantive review, by:

(A) Conducting research or making any
investigation which requires the exercise of
judgment on the part of the official or
designated employee and the purpose of which
is to influence the decision; or

(B) Preparing or presenting any report,
analysis or opinion, orally or in writing,
which requires the exercise of judgment on the
part of the official or designated employee
and the purpose of which is to influence the
decision.

Regulation 18700(c).

With regard to a governmental decision which is within or
before an official's agency or an agency appointed by subject
to the budgetary control of his or her agency an official is
attempting to use his or her official position to influence the
decision if, for the purpose of influencing the decision, the
official contacts, or appears before, or otherwise attempts to
influence any member, officer, employee or consultant of the
agency. Attempts to influence include, but are not limited to,
appearances or contacts by the official on behalf of a business
entity, client, or customer. (Regulation 18700.1.)

Accordingly, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
economic interest of Chairperson Winter will be materially

___________________________
4 Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.

Our File No. I-91-554
Page 5


affected by a governmental decision, she must not only disqualify
herself from participating in formal decisions of the Napa County
Conservation, Development and Planning Commission which may affect
such interest, but she must also abstain from attempting to
influence such decisions by communicating with other members of
the commission or the staff regarding the decisions.

Foreseeability

The effect of a decision is reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. To be
foreseeable, the effect of a decision must be more than a mere
possibility; however, certainty is not required. (Downey cases v.
Downey Community Development Comm. (1987) 196 Cal.App.3d 983, 989-
991; Witt v. Morrow (1977) 70 Cal.App.3d 817; In re Thorner (1975)
1 FPPC Ops. 198.) The Act seeks to prevent more than actual
conflicts of interest; it seeks to prevent even the appearance of
a possible conflict of interest. (Witt v. Morrow, supra at 823.)

It is reasonably foreseeable that the proposed ordinance will
have a financial effect on the property owned by Doctors,
Chairperson Winter's source of income. The effect need not be a
benefit in order to be subject to the rules regarding conflict of
interest; a detrimental effect is also a financial effect under
the Act.

Materiality

Even if a decision will have a foreseeable financial effect
on Doctors, Chairperson Winter will not have to disqualify herself
from participation unless the financial effect on Doctors is
material. The facts indicate that Doctors will not be directly
involved in the inclusionary housing ordinance decision (see
Regulation 18702.1(b)); therefore it is necessary to determine
whether the financial effect on Doctors meets the appropriate
threshold as set forth in Regulation 18702.2 for business entities
indirectly involved in governmental decisions.

Regulation 18702.2 determines materiality using the size of a
business as the basic criterion and then setting forth various
dollar amounts as threshold amounts for the differing size
categories. We have not been given facts sufficient to determine
the category into which Doctors falls, but given the annual
revenues of $150,000,000, we suggest you look at 18702.2(f).
Subdivision (f), which concerns business entities with net
tangible assets of at least $4,000,000 and pre-tax income for the
last fiscal year of at least $750,000, with net income from that
period of at least $400,000, utilizes the following criteria for
materiality:

(1) The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal year
of $30,000 or more; or

Our File No. I-91-554
Page 6


(2) The decision will result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a
fiscal year in the amount of $7,500 or more; or

(3) The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the value of assets or liabilities
of $30,000 or more.

Since we have not been provided with requisite facts
pertaining to Doctors, we cannot determine whether the effect of
the inclusionary housing ordinance on Doctors will be material.
It will be up to Chairperson Winter to obtain the information
needed for application of Regulation 18702.2.

Public Generally

Even if it is ascertained that the effect of the proposed
ordinance will have a material financial effect on Doctors,
Ms. Winter may participate in the decision if the effect on
Doctors is indistinguishable from the effect on the public
generally. (Section 87103.) Regulation 18703 clarifies the
"public generally" exception and provides, in pertinent part, as
follows:

A material financial effect of a governmental
decision on an official's interests, as described
in Government Code Section 87103, is
distinguishable from its effect on the public
generally unless the decision will affect the
official's interest in substantially the same
manner as it will affect all members of the public
or a significant segment of the public.

The "public" is comprised of all persons residing, owning
property or doing business in the jurisdiction of the agency in
question. (In re Legon (1985) 9 FPPC Ops. 1.) In the case of the
Napa County Conservation, Development and Planning Commission, the
jurisdiction would be Napa County. Consequently, for the public
generally exception to apply, a decision that would materially
affect Doctors would have to affect a significant segment of the
public of Napa County in substantially the same manner. (Dood
Advice Letter, No. A-88-214; Burnham Advice Letter, No. A-86-210.)

The Commission has never adopted a strict arithmetic test for
determining what constitutes a significant segment of the public.
However, in order to apply the public generally exception, the
population affected must be large in number and heterogeneous in
nature. (In re Ferraro (1978) 4 FPPC Ops. 62.)

Our File No. I-91-554
Page 7


In addition to comprising a significant segment of the
jurisdiction, the group affected must be affected in a
substantially similar way. We have not been provided with facts
that would indicate that a significant segment of the public in
Napa County would be affected in a manner similar to the effect on
Doctors by the inclusionary housing ordinance. Hence, we cannot
determine that the "publicly generally" exception would apply in
Charpentier's situation. If you can provide relevant
statistics regarding the effect of the proposed ordinance on the
property owners of Napa County, we will be happy to explore then
"publicly generally" exception further as it pertains to Kathryn
Winter's source of income. However, without further information
we cannot presume to determine that the "publicly generally"
exception is appropriate as to the effect on Doctors.

I trust the above answers your question. If you need further
information, please do not hesitate to call me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
[illegible signature]

By: Susan L. Borrow
Counsel, Legal Division

The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act require Chairperson Winter to disqualify herself from participating in

decisions regarding the proposed ordinance if it is reasonably foreseeable that the pending governmental decisions will have a material financial effect on her economic interests, as discussed below.

Kathryn J. Winter is Chairperson of the Napa County Conservation, Development and Planning Commission. Chairperson Winter’s husband, Mick Winter, is employed as a systems analyst for The Doctors’ Company ("Doctors"), a physician-owned nonprofit medical malpractice insurer. Doctors, a company with annual revenues of approximately one hundred fifty million dollars, has offices in Napa County. The Napa County Conservation, Development and Planning Commission is currently scheduled to consider whether to recommend to the Napa County Board of Supervisors that an inclusionary housing ordinance be developed and adopted as part of a comprehensive response to this aspect of the county’s general plan. Such a recommendation, if made, could include endorsement of a model ordinance requiring some or all of the following: (1) that a certain percentage of all new residential units constructed in a project be "affordable" as that term is defined in the ordinance; (2) that a fee be paid to an affordable housing fund in lieu of the actual construction of affordable units; or (3) that construction of a certain number of affordable units take place off-site. In addition, the Napa County Conservation, Development and Planning Commission is likely to recommend the creation of additional sources of funding for affordable housing through means such as assessment of linkage fees from developers of agricultural, commercial and industrial projects based upon project-generated employment, imposition of a business license tax, or increase in documentary transfer or sales taxes. sections.analysis:
Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making, participating in, or using their official position to influence a governmental decision in which they know or have reason to know they have a financial interest. Chairperson Winter is a public official. (Section 82048.)

Financial Interests

An official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, or on a member of the official’s immediate family,3 or on:

(c) Any source of income, other than gifts and other than loans by a commercial lending institution in the regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value provided to, received by or promised to the public official within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made.

Section 87103.

Chairperson Winter has a potentially disqualifying economic interest under Section 87103(c). This interest must be evaluated to determine whether she must disqualify herself from participating in governmental decisions regarding the proposed ordinance.

Section 82030 defines "income," in pertinent part, as follows:

"Income" means, except as provided in subdivision (b), a payment received, including but not limited to any salary, wage, advance, dividend, interest, rent, proceeds from any sale, gift, including any gift of food or beverage, loan, forgiveness or payment of indebtedness received by the filer, reimbursement for expenses, per diem, or contribution to an insurance or pension program paid by any person other than an employer, and including any community property interest in the income of a spouse.

Thus the salary earned by Mick Winter from his employer, Doctors, would be considered income to Chairperson Winter, and Doctors would then be a source of income as contemplated by Section 87103(c), quoted above. We are assuming that Doctors paid Mr. Winter at least $500 in the last twelve months, so that Chairperson Winter would have a community property interest of at least $250 in her husband’s salary.

3 An official’s "immediate family" are his or her spouse and dependent children. (Section 82029.)

Making, Participating in Making, or Attempting to Influence a Governmental Decision

A public official makes a governmental decision or participates in the making of a governmental decision whenever the public official votes on a matter, commits the agency to a course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of the agency. (Regulation 18700(b).) Additionally, a public official participates in a governmental decision when, acting within the authority of his or her position, the public official:

(1) Negotiates, without significant substantive review, with a governmental entity or private person regarding the decision; or

(2) Advises or makes recommendations to the decision-maker, either directly or without significant intervening substantive review, by:

(A) Conducting research or making any investigation which requires the exercise of judgment on the part of the official or designated employee and the purpose of which is to influence the decision; or

(B) Preparing or presenting any report, analysis or opinion, orally or in writing, which requires the exercise of judgment on the part of the official or designated employee and the purpose of which is to influence the decision.

Regulation 18700(c).

With regard to a governmental decision which is within or before an official's agency or an agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary control of his or her agency, an official is attempting to use his or her official position to influence the decision if, for the purpose of influencing the decision, the official contacts, or appears before, or otherwise attempts to influence any member, officer, employee or consultant of the agency. Attempts to influence include, but are not limited to, appearances or contacts by the official on behalf of a business entity, client, or customer. (Regulation 18700.1.)

Accordingly, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the economic interest of Chairperson Winter will be materially

4 Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a cost of 10¢ per page.

affected by a governmental decision, she must not only disqualify herself from participating in formal decisions of the Napa County Conservation, Development and Planning Commission which may affect such interest, but she must also abstain from attempting to influence such decisions by communicating with other members of the commission or the staff regarding the decisions.

Foreseeability

The effect of a decision is reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. To be foreseeable, the effect of a decision must be more than a mere possibility; however, certainty is not required. (Downey Cares v. Downey Community Development Comm. (1987) 196 Cal.App.3d 983, 989-991; Witt v. Morrow (1977) 70 Cal.App.3d 817; In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.) The Act seeks to prevent more than actual conflicts of interest; it seeks to prevent even the appearance of a possible conflict of interest. (Witt v. Morrow, supra at 823.)

It is reasonably foreseeable that the proposed ordinance will have a financial effect on the property owned by Doctors, Chairperson Winter’s source of income. The effect need not be a benefit in order to be subject to the rules regarding conflict of interest; a detrimental effect is also a financial effect under the Act.

Materiality

Even if a decision will have a foreseeable financial effect on Doctors, Chairperson Winter will not have to disqualify herself from participation unless the financial effect on Doctors is material. The facts indicate that Doctors will not be directly involved in the inclusionary housing ordinance decision (see Regulation 18702.1(b)); therefore it is necessary to determine whether the financial effect on Doctors meets the appropriate threshold as set forth in Regulation 18702.2 for business entities indirectly involved in governmental decisions.

Regulation 18702.2 determines materiality using the size of a business as the basic criterion and then setting forth various dollar amounts as threshold amounts for the differing size categories. We have not been given facts sufficient to determine the category into which Doctors falls, but given the annual revenues of $150,000,000, we suggest you look at 18702.2(f). Subdivision (f), which concerns business entities with net tangible assets of at least $4,000,000 and pre-tax income for the last fiscal year of at least $750,000, with net income from that period of at least $400,000, utilizes the following criteria for materiality:

(1) The decision will result in an increase or decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal year of $30,000 or more; or

(2) The decision will result in the business entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $7,500 or more; or

(3) The decision will result in an increase or decrease in the value of assets or liabilities of $30,000 or more.

Since we have not been provided with requisite facts pertaining to Doctors, we cannot determine whether the effect of the inclusionary housing ordinance on Doctors will be material. It will be up to Chairperson Winter to obtain the information needed for application of Regulation 18702.2.

Public Generally

Even if it is ascertained that the effect of the proposed ordinance will have a material financial effect on Doctors, Ms. Winter may participate in the decision if the effect on Doctors is indistinguishable from the effect on the public generally. (Section 87103.) Regulation 18703 clarifies the "public generally" exception and provides, in pertinent part, as follows:

A material financial effect of a governmental decision on an official's interests, as described in Government Code Section 87103, is distinguishable from its effect on the public generally unless the decision will affect the official's interest in substantially the same manner as it will affect all members of the public or a significant segment of the public.

The "public" is comprised of all the persons residing, owning property or doing business in the jurisdiction of the agency in question. (In re Legan (1985) 9 FPPC Ops. 1.) In the case of the Napa County Conservation, Development and Planning Commission, the jurisdiction would be Napa County. Consequently, for the "public generally" exception to apply, a decision that would materially affect Doctors would have to affect a significant segment of the public of Napa County in substantially the same manner. (Dowd Advice Letter, No. A-88-214; Burnham Advice Letter, No. A-86-210.)

The Commission has never adopted a strict arithmetic test for determining what constitutes a significant segment of the public. However, in order to apply the public generally exception, the population affected must be large in number and heterogeneous in nature. (In re Ferraro (1978) 4 FPPC Ops. 62.)

In addition to comprising a significant segment of the jurisdiction, the group affected must be affected in a substantially similar way. We have not been provided with facts that would indicate that a significant segment of the public in Napa County would be affected in a manner similar to the effect on Doctors by the inclusionary housing ordinance. Hence, we cannot determine that the "public generally" exception would apply in Chairperson Winter's situation. If you can provide relevant statistics regarding the effect of the proposed ordinance on the property owners of Napa County, we will be happy to explore the "public generally" exception further as it pertains to Kathryn Winter's source of income. However, without further information we cannot presently determine that the "public generally" exception is appropriate as to the effect on Doctors.

I trust the above answers your question. If you need further information, please do not hesitate to call me at (916) 322-5901.

Winter to disqualify herself from participating in decisions regarding an inclusionary housing ordinance which would encourage the provision of affordable housing in connection with the development of residential projects?

decisions regarding the proposed ordinance if it is reasonably foreseeable that the pending governmental decisions will have a material financial effect on her economic interests, as discussed below.
